authoringProvenance.author: OpenAI GPT-5.6 Pro — candidate authoring assistant

items.prompt: A finance tool stores one immutable daily net-flow value for a year and must answer thousands of questions such as 'What was the total flow from day l through day r?' Which prior strategy decision transfers most directly?
constraints: Daily values are immutable.
Queries are numerous.
Each query asks for an additive date interval.

options.text: Store each date in a hash set and use exact date membership as the reusable operation for computing every requested interval total.
Treat days as a static sequence, build cumulative totals once, and recover each interval by subtracting two prefixes.
Sort the daily totals by value, then answer each requested date interval from the corresponding segment of the sorted sequence.

feedback.reason: The decisive structure is a static additive range query, independent of the finance vocabulary. Cumulative state supports repeated interval totals.
feedback.details: The surface form uses days and cash flow, but the decision contract is the same as an immutable array with many additive range queries. A prefix total preserves the sum of every leading interval, and subtraction cancels the shared earlier days. Sorting would destroy calendar contiguity, while a set answers presence rather than an aggregate. The transfer boundary is that updates, non-additive queries, or output requiring individual transactions would change the mechanism. The general rule is to map the new story to structure, operation, and lifecycle before reusing a strategy.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.sort_transactions: Ordering by value breaks the date interval that defines each query. Sorting becomes relevant only for value-rank or threshold questions that do not depend on original chronology.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.hash_dates: A date set can answer whether a day exists, but not the sum over a range. It becomes useful if the query primitive changes to exact membership.

items.prompt: A venue receives badge IDs one at a time and must immediately flag an ID that has appeared earlier in the same event. Which strategy-selection rule transfers?
constraints: Badge IDs arrive one at a time.
The answer is required immediately.
The question is exact, not approximate.

options.text: Wait until the event ends, sort all IDs, and then report duplicates.
Keep pointers at the first and latest badge because two pointers work on sequences.
Maintain a set of previously seen IDs and test membership before insertion.

feedback.reason: The underlying contract is online exact membership over a growing prefix. Retained seen-state supports the required answer time.
feedback.details: Badge IDs are merely keys. The decisive signals are that input arrives incrementally, an answer is required before future data is known, and prior membership is the complete query primitive. A set stores exactly the state needed to detect repetition. End-of-event sorting could answer an offline audit but misses immediate alerts, and pointer placement has no ordered discard rule. The transfer boundary is memory or expiration: a bounded recent-history rule would require state eviction, while approximate membership would require an explicitly relaxed correctness contract.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.sort_at_close: This is a legal offline audit candidate but violates the immediate-answer timing. It becomes appropriate when alerts may be delayed until the full event log is available.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.two_end_badges: A sequence alone does not supply the ordering invariant needed for pointer elimination. Two pointers become useful only after an ordered relation or window contract is established.

items.prompt: A production line must choose the minimum integer machine speed that completes all jobs before a deadline. A deterministic check can decide whether a proposed speed is sufficient. Which strategy-selection rule transfers?
constraints: Speed is an integer in a known bounded range.
Feasibility can be checked for a proposed speed.
Greater speed never reduces completed work.

options.text: Sort jobs by duration; sorting alone always reveals the minimum speed.
Prove that any speed above a feasible speed is also feasible, then binary-search the first feasible speed.
Insert tried speeds into a set and choose the smallest stored value.

feedback.reason: The unfamiliar surface still exposes an ordered answer domain and a monotonic feasibility predicate, which is the decision boundary for binary search on answer.
feedback.details: The target is not an existing element but the smallest parameter satisfying a constraint. If increasing speed cannot make completion harder, feasibility is upward-closed: insufficient speeds form a prefix and sufficient speeds a suffix. That boundary licenses half discard, while the total cost is logarithmic in the speed range times the check cost. Sorting jobs may be part of the feasibility check but does not by itself search the speed domain. The transfer boundary is nonmonotonic behaviour, such as speeds that introduce failures or discrete modes where faster can become infeasible.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.sort_jobs_only: Job ordering may help evaluate one candidate speed, but it does not locate the minimum parameter without a search or derivation. It becomes complete only if a proof directly computes the optimum after sorting.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.hash_speeds: A set records which speeds were tried but provides no rule for choosing informative trials or proving untried values impossible. It becomes relevant only as memoization inside another search.

items.prompt: Daily advertising costs are nonnegative. A campaign planner wants the longest consecutive run whose total cost is at most B. Which strategy-selection rule transfers, and what boundary must be remembered?
constraints: Costs are nonnegative.
The selected days must be consecutive.
The goal is maximum length under total cost B.

options.text: Sort days from cheapest to most expensive, then choose the longest affordable prefix and treat those selected days as the campaign run.
Use prefix sums because consecutive-range tasks are solved by cumulative totals, and query-time arithmetic will reveal the longest affordable run.
Use a variable window because expanding cannot reduce cost and shrinking cannot increase it; negative rebates would break this movement rule.

feedback.reason: Nonnegative contributions make window validity repairable by forward boundary movement. The transfer depends on that sign constraint, not on the advertising vocabulary.
feedback.details: The output is one contiguous interval, and every added day can only increase or preserve total cost. When the budget is exceeded, advancing the left boundary is the only way to reduce the sum, so no longer valid interval starting earlier is skipped. Each boundary moves forward at most n times. Prefix sums can evaluate a chosen interval but do not alone identify the longest one in linear time, and sorting destroys chronology. The transfer boundary is explicit: negative rebates or another nonmonotone contribution can make shrinking increase the total and invalidate the standard window proof.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.prefix_always: Prefix sums provide range totals but do not automatically choose optimal boundaries. They become the preferred mechanism when query volume, negative values, or another search structure changes the decision.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.sort_days: Sorting changes which days are consecutive and therefore changes the answer object. It becomes legal only for a noncontiguous selection problem.

items.prompt: For each building in a street, return the first building to its right that is taller. Which prior mechanism transfers despite the new surface?
constraints: Return the first taller building to the right.
Original street order must be preserved.
Each building needs at most one witness.

options.text: Sort buildings by height, then use adjacent taller values to determine the first greater building for every original street position.
Maintain a decreasing stack of unresolved building indices; a taller building resolves and pops shorter indices.
Push every building onto an ordinary stack and never remove anything until the end.

feedback.reason: The task is a next-greater relation. A monotonic stack keeps exactly the unresolved frontier and discards entries when their first valid witness appears.
feedback.details: Street position supplies the left-to-right processing order, while height supplies the comparison. Indices on the stack have not yet seen a taller building; when a new height exceeds the top, that new index is the first valid witness because all intermediate buildings have already been processed. Popped indices are resolved permanently, giving amortized O(n) work. An ordinary unpruned stack does not encode the dominance relation, and sorting loses 'first to the right.' The transfer boundary is nearest/first witness semantics; asking for every taller building would require more output and invalidate permanent discard.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.ordinary_history: Retaining history without a monotonic invariant provides no efficient resolution rule. It becomes useful only if another mechanism searches that history or if LIFO order is itself the output contract.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.sort_heights: Height order does not preserve street order or nearest-right identity. Sorting becomes relevant for global rank questions rather than directional nearest witnesses.

items.prompt: Shipment weights are already sorted. The system must determine whether two distinct shipments exactly fill capacity C. Which prior strategy-selection rule transfers most directly?
constraints: Weights are already sorted.
Two distinct shipments are required.
Only existence is requested.

options.text: For every shipment, binary-search its complement in the remaining sorted weights; this is the only strategy that should exploit the order.
Build a hash set because pair-sum tasks require complement lookup, even when the weights are already sorted and can support ordered elimination.
Use opposite-end pointers; a sum below C eliminates the current lightest shipment, and a sum above C eliminates the current heaviest.

feedback.reason: Sorted order makes the sum comparison a safe endpoint-elimination rule, so the pair-sum invariant transfers directly.
feedback.details: The logistics vocabulary changes none of the structural facts: there is an ordered sequence, a two-element sum target, and an existence output. The lightest remaining item cannot reach the target with any smaller partner when the current maximum partner is still too small; the symmetric argument removes the heaviest when the sum is too large. Hashing and repeated binary search can be legal alternatives, but the existing order makes a single O(n) pointer pass with O(1) extra state especially direct. The transfer boundary is lost ordering, original-index output constraints, or a different relation than monotone sum comparison.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.hash_required: Hashing is a candidate, not a universal requirement. It becomes preferable when the input is unsorted, expected-time lookup is acceptable, and extra memory fits.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.binary_each_only: Repeated binary search is legal on sorted data but costs O(n log n) and duplicates searches. It becomes relevant when pointer monotonicity is unavailable or the query structure differs.

items.prompt: A dashboard asks many range questions, but values are updated after nearly every query and each query asks for the median of the current range. Why does a static prefix-sum decision not transfer merely from the word range?
constraints: Values change after nearly every query.
Queries ask for medians, not sums.
The question tests transfer boundary, not replacement implementation.

options.text: Reuse prefix sums because the large query count makes preprocessing valuable, even if updates and median output change what the stored state must support.
Reuse prefix sums unchanged because the selected positions are still ranges, so the familiar range-query label is enough to preserve the original strategy.
Both the update lifecycle and the non-cancellable median aggregate differ, so the stored prefix-sum invariant cannot answer the new contract.

feedback.reason: Transfer follows preserved mechanism and preconditions, not shared vocabulary. Frequent updates and median queries remove the static additive cancellation that prefix sums require.
feedback.details: The original prefix-sum rule relied on two facts: the data snapshot stayed stable, and the aggregate could be isolated by subtracting cumulative totals. Here almost every update makes a static prefix array stale, and median has no subtraction operation that removes an earlier prefix while retaining order statistics. Many queries increase the value of some reusable structure, but not this insufficient representation. The transfer rule is to re-check output, update model, state sufficiency, and invariant even when the new prompt uses familiar nouns.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.same_range: Range describes the selected positions, not the aggregate or update lifecycle. The claim becomes true only when the new operation and stability assumptions match the original invariant.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.more_queries: Query volume justifies evaluating preprocessing, but cannot make an incompatible representation correct. It becomes relevant after a legal update-aware median mechanism is identified.
